AktAcc_explanation: The passive construction 'has been prepared' denotes a durative process of preparation directed toward a defined result state (readiness for those whom it is intended), satisfying the criterion of a gradual change-of-state culminating in a defined result condition.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has been prepared' denotes a punctual transition into a new result state (the state of being prepared/ready), can be modified by a specific time-point adverbial (e.g., 'has been prepared since the beginning'), and entails a definable threshold crossing from unprepared to prepared, satisfying the achievement criteria.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has been prepared' denotes a bounded event with an inherent endpoint and result state (something is prepared/completed), not a dynamic durative activity without inherent completion.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has been prepared' entails a resulting change of state (something transitions from unprepared to prepared), which disqualifies it from being a semelfactive; it is a resultative event.

DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'It is for those for whom it has been prepared by my Father' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal temporal adverbial that anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The passive perfect 'has been prepared' entails a change of state (seats being prepared/made ready) where that resulting state of readiness persists at the reference time, matching the set_to_0_if criterion for resulting states that hold at reference time.
DtRes_explanation: The clause uses a stative copular construction ('has been prepared') that describes a state rather than encoding a transition; it reports a past preparation without indicating that a resulting state currently holds at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a completed action of preparation (transition into a prepared state) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, and lacks duration adverbials indicating continuous extension to the reference time.

VcPass_explanation: The target 'has been prepared' instantiates VcPass: it has the form 'have' + 'been' + past participle of transitive verb 'prepare', the grammatical subject 'it' is the semantic patient (the thing prepared), and the agent is expressed in the by-phrase 'by my Father'.

FullText: » Have you any idea what these people are saying ? « Jesus answered : » Yes . It is written , ‘ From the lips of children and babies at the breast you have made your praise complete . ’ «

AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have made your praise complete' entails a resulting change of state (praise transitioning from incomplete to complete), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have made' denotes a bounded dynamic event involving change-of-state (causing praise to become complete), which involves internal eventive unfolding and progression rather than a static condition.

DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a resultant state achieved through a completed action (making praise complete), focusing on the transition to a new state rather than a situation holding continuously over time.

FullText: Jesus said to them : » Have you read the Scriptures , ‘ The stone the builders rejected has been made the chief cornerstone of the building . This was Jehovah’s doing , and it is wonderful in our eyes . ’

ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'read' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent 'you' who performs the reading action), making it unergative/transitive rather than unaccusative.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'read' licenses an internal argument ('the Scriptures'), making it transitive and thus not an unergative one-participant event.

AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have read' denotes a completed perceptual/cognitive event of accessing and processing a bounded text (the Scriptures), which entails a specific result state (having acquired knowledge of the content) and is not a dynamic, durative activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The perfective aspect 'have read' entails a resulting state of completion (knowledge acquired from reading) and does not construe the event as a single punctual occurrence, thus failing the semelfactive criteria.
AktState_explanation: The perfect aspect 'have read' denotes a bounded perceptual/cognitive event marking the completion of the action of reading, which is a change-of-state from not having read to having read, thus encoding eventive change rather than a static stative condition.

DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'Have you read the Scriptures' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal temporal adverbial that anchors the event to a specific past time interval.

DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have read' reports a past interaction (reading the Scriptures) without entailing a persisting resulting state; it is an experiential perfect asking whether the addressees have encountered the text, not whether they currently possess knowledge that holds at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'Have you read the Scriptures' describes a discrete bounded event (the completion of reading) rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span, with no duration adverbials indicating continuous extension to the reference time.

TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have read' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by past participle 'read' with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the simple present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have read' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'you' as the semantic agent (doer), not a passive construction with 'have been' + past participle.

FullText: Jesus said to them : » Have you read the Scriptures , ‘ The stone the builders rejected has been made the chief cornerstone of the building . This was Jehovah’s doing , and it is wonderful in our eyes . ’

FullText: » Again he sent out other servants with orders to say to the guests , ‘ I have prepared my feast . Oxen and fat livestock are butchered and everything is ready , so come to the feast . ’

AktSemel_explanation: The present perfect 'have prepared' entails a resulting change of state (the feast is now prepared/ready), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status according to the set_to_0_if criterion.

DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have prepared' describes a change of state (from unprepared to prepared feast), and the context 'everything is ready' explicitly indicates that the resulting state (the feast being prepared and ready) holds at the reference time.

VcPass_explanation: The grammatical subject 'I' corresponds to the semantic agent (the one who prepared the feast), and 'have prepared' is active voice with a direct object 'my feast', not a passive construction with 'have been' + past participle.

FullText: The Pharisees assembled together when they heard that he had put the Sadducees to silence .

ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'put to silence' licenses an external argument (the subject 'he') that acts as an agent/initiator causing the Sadducees (direct object) to be silenced, thus failing the unaccusative criteria.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'put to silence' licenses an internal argument (the Sadducees as direct object), thus failing the requirement for ASUnrg which demands no internal argument be licensed or realized.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'put to silence' denotes a punctual boundary transition (moving from a state of speaking to a state of silence) without a durative process phase, making it an achievement rather than an accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'put to silence' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (the Sadducees becoming silent), naturally modifiable by time-point adverbials (e.g., 'at that moment'), lacks measurable internal duration, and represents a definable threshold crossing rather than an unfolding process.

AktSemel_explanation: The event 'put to silence' entails a resulting change of state (the Sadducees transitioning from speaking to being silenced), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'put to silence' denotes a bounded change-of-state event (causing the Sadducees to become silent), involving dynamic progression from one state to another, which is incompatible with AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'he had put the Sadducees to silence' uses past perfect morphology to express temporal anteriority within a narrative (the silencing occurred before the Pharisees assembled), without any indication that the situation is contrary to fact; it describes an event that actually occurred.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had put to silence' is not negated; the sentence is positive and describes an event that occurred, with no negator ('not', 'n't', 'never', or negative quantifier) modifying the target gram.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause contains the clausal past time anchor 'when they heard', a temporal conjunction that explicitly anchors the event 'had put to silence' to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'put to silence' entails a change of state (the Sadducees becoming silenced), and this resulting state is relevant to the narrative context—it motivates the Pharisees' assembly in response to this accomplished silencing.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'put to silence' describes a change of state where the Sadducees transitioned from speaking to being silenced, and the resulting state (silence) is entailed to hold at the reference time when the Pharisees heard about it.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (putting the Sadducees to silence) rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span, and the verb entails a transition into a new state.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'put to silence' is a transition event (causing a change of state from speaking to silent) construed as a completed whole in past time, and the clause functions as a discrete bounded occurrence in the narrative sequence.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had put to silence' is a past perfect form asserting a realized fact in the actual world (the Pharisees heard that he had silenced the Sadducees), with no modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical context present.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is morphologically past, making this a past perfect form rather than a simple present perfect, which satisfies the set_to_1_if condition for past auxiliary marking.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'had put to silence' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'he' as the semantic agent (doer) and the object 'the Sadducees' as the semantic patient; it lacks the passive auxiliary sequence 'have/had + been + past participle'.

FullText: » Woe to you , scribes and Pharisees , hypocrites ! You tithe mint and dill and cumin , and have left undone the weightier matters of the law : justice , mercy and faith . You should do both and leave nothing undone .
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'have left undone' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('You') and an overt direct object ('the weightier matters of the law'), satisfying the two-participant transitive pattern with external and internal arguments.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'have left undone' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent 'you'), as 'leave' is an agentive verb requiring an external argument to perform the action of leaving something undone.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'have left undone' licenses an internal argument ('the weightier matters of the law'), making it transitive and thus not an unergative one-participant event.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'have left undone' denotes a punctual state of neglect or omission (a boundary transition into a condition of incompleteness) rather than a durative process directed toward a defined endpoint, and lacks the inherent process phase characteristic of accomplishments.
AktAch_explanation: The phrase 'have left undone' denotes a punctual transition into a state of neglect or omission (crossing the threshold from doing to not-doing), naturally modifiable by time-point adverbials (e.g., 'have left undone at that moment'), and does not permit interruption as an ongoing process.
AktAct_explanation: The phrase 'have left undone' denotes a bounded result state (the weightier matters remain in an undone condition) with an inherent endpoint, not a dynamic durative activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have left undone' entails a resulting change of state (the matters remain in an undone condition) and does not constitute a single punctual occurrence, thus failing the semelfactive criteria.
AktState_explanation: The verb phrase 'have left undone' denotes a bounded change-of-state event (the action of neglecting/failing to do something), involving dynamic progression from a prior state of engagement to a state of non-completion, which is incompatible with AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'have left undone the weightier matters of the law' presents a situation that has actually occurred in the actual world (the scribes and Pharisees have in fact neglected these matters), without any indication that it is contrary to fact or conditional.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have left undone' is negated by the prefix 'un-' in 'undone', which construes the weightier matters as not being done (absent from reality), satisfying the ClNegPol feature.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'have left undone the weightier matters of the law' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal temporal adverbial that anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'have left undone' entails a change of state (the weightier matters remain in an undone condition at the reference time), which is the relevant result state for the subsequent moral judgment, making this non-experiential.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have left undone' describes a change of state (from done to undone) where the weightier matters of the law remain in that undone state at the reference time, satisfying the resultative condition.
DtUniv_explanation: The phrase 'have left undone' describes a discrete bounded event (the action of neglecting/omitting the weightier matters) rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span, and the verb entails a transition into a state of neglect rather than duration.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'have left undone' presents a completed transition (the action of leaving matters undone) as a bounded whole, construed as a discrete occurrence in the narrative sequence of accusations, matching the set_to_1_if criterion for transition events asserted as completed wholes.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have left undone' asserts a realized fact about the Pharisees' actual behavior (they have neglected weightier matters), not a projected, hypothetical, or desired situation.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have left undone' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by a past participle ('left'), with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'have left undone' is active voice with 'left' as the past participle functioning as a transitive verb taking 'the weightier matters' as its grammatical object (semantic patient), not as a passive auxiliary construction with 'been' + past participle.

FullText: » You claim that you would not have been involved with your fathers when they spilled the blood of the prophets .
ASTrns_explanation: The passive construction 'would not have been involved with your fathers' licenses both an external argument (the subject 'you' as the affected patient) and an internal argument (the prepositional phrase 'with your fathers' functioning as a participant in the event), satisfying the passive voice criterion for ASTrns.
ASUncc_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive ('been involved') with a suppressed external argument (the agent who would involve the subject), and passive clauses with licensed external arguments are marked as 0 per the set_to_0_if criteria.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'involved' licenses an internal argument ('with your fathers'), making it transitive and thus not a one-participant unergative event.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'be involved' denotes a state or condition of participation rather than a durative process directed toward a defined endpoint; it lacks the inherent culmination structure required for AktAcc and is better characterized as a stative achievement marking entry into a state.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'be involved' denotes a durative state or condition that unfolds over measurable duration rather than a punctual transition into a new state, and does not naturally modify with time-point adverbials in the way achievements do.
AktAct_explanation: The phrase 'would not have been involved with' denotes a bounded, completed state of non-participation in a specific past event (the spilling of blood), not a dynamic, durative activity without inherent endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'been involved' entails a resulting state (involvement/participation) and does not constitute a single punctual dynamic occurrence, thus it is not semelfactive.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'would not have been involved' is morphologically marked with progressive/perfect aspect ('have been') indicating a bounded eventive state of participation in a dynamic action, which entails internal development and change rather than a static condition.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram occurs in the apodosis of a counterfactual conditional introduced by 'would not have been involved', expressing an unfulfilled hypothetical situation contrary to the actual world where the speaker claims the addressee would not have participated in their fathers' actions.
ClNegPol_explanation: The auxiliary 'would' is negated with 'not' in the form 'would not have been involved', directly negating the eventuality of being involved.
DtDTAM_explanation: The target clause 'would not have been involved' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal temporal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The negative perfect 'would not have been involved' functions as a claim about the absence of an event in the subject's life history, matching the set_to_1_if criterion for negative Perfects, and focuses on whether the experience occurred rather than on any resulting state.
DtRes_explanation: The target is a counterfactual conditional (would not have been involved) expressing a hypothetical claim about a past situation, not a change of state with a resulting state currently in effect; the copular construction 'been involved' describes a state rather than encoding a transition into that state.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a counterfactual conditional state (would not have been involved) focused on non-participation in a discrete bounded event (spilling blood), not a continuous situation extending to reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'would not have been involved' is a negated passive construction with 'have been' + past participle that expresses a counterfactual conditional state rather than asserting a bounded completed event, and the negation combined with the conditional modal 'would' leaves the situation open and unrealized rather than presenting it as a bounded whole.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram contains the modal auxiliary 'would' combined with the perfect infinitive 'have been involved', which presents a counterfactual or hypothetical situation rather than an asserted realized fact.
TLTS_explanation: The gram contains a modal auxiliary 'would' preceding 'have', which instantiates the TLTS feature according to the set_to_1_if rule for modal auxiliaries preceding 'have'.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'would not have been involved' instantiates VcPass: it has the form auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle ('involved') of a transitive verb, the grammatical subject 'you' corresponds to the semantic patient (the entity affected by involvement), and the agent is expressed in a 'with'-phrase ('with your fathers').

FullText: » Know this , if the master of the house had known what time the thief was coming , he would have watched and his house would not have been vandalized .
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'had known' introduces an overt internal argument ('what time the thief was coming'), satisfying the condition for a two-participant transitive event with an external argument (master) and an internal argument (the content clause functioning as the direct object).
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'know' licenses an external argument (the master as agent/experiencer) and denotes a cognitive state with an external argument, not a one-participant unaccusative event.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'had known' licenses an internal argument (the clause 'what time the thief was coming'), making it transitive and thus not an unergative one-participant event.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'had known' denotes a punctual cognitive achievement—a single transition into a state of awareness or understanding—rather than a durative process culminating in a result state, and lacks an inherent endpoint or measurable duration toward completion.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'had known' denotes a durative state of possessing knowledge rather than a punctual transition into a new state; knowing unfolds over measurable duration and does not represent crossing a definable threshold at a temporal boundary.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'had known' denotes a bounded perceptual/cognitive event marking the onset of awareness of a specific fact (what time the thief was coming), which is a single completed occurrence rather than a dynamic, durative activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had known' entails a resulting state of possessing knowledge, which is a change of state from not knowing to knowing, thus it does not qualify as a semelfactive punctual occurrence without consequent result.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'had known' denotes a mental state (knowledge) that would hold over time without dynamic change or progression, meeting the criterion for stative aspect despite its past perfect morphology.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram 'had known' occurs in the protasis of a counterfactual conditional ('if the master of the house had known...') paired with apodosis containing modal perfects ('would have watched', 'would not have been vandalized'), indicating the condition was not satisfied in fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had known' is in a conditional clause expressing a hypothetical situation; the negation 'not' in the main clause negates 'would have been vandalized', not the target gram itself.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'had known' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal temporal adverbial anchoring it to a specific past time interval; the 'if' is a conditional marker, not a temporal anchor.
DtExp_explanation: The negative conditional perfect 'had known' functions as a claim about the absence of an event in the subject's history (the master did not know), fitting the experiential feature without entailing a resulting state that persists at reference time.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'had known' is in a counterfactual conditional clause (contrary-to-fact) expressing what did not happen; it does not entail a resulting state that holds at the reference time, as the condition was never met.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a hypothetical transition into a state of knowledge ('had known') rather than a situation holding continuously over time; it focuses on the acquisition of knowledge at a specific point, not duration.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'know' is stative and denotes a state of awareness without a construed boundary or transition; the clause does not present a bounded occurrence but rather a hypothetical state that would have held at a past time.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had known' appears in a conditional protasis ('if the master of the house had known...') paired with a counterfactual apodosis ('he would have watched'), presenting a hypothetical, non-realized situation rather than an asserted fact.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary is morphologically past ('had' + past participle 'known'), which instantiates TLTS according to the set_to_1_if rule for past perfect forms.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had known' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'master' as the semantic agent (doer), not a passive construction with auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle.

FullText: » Know this , if the master of the house had known what time the thief was coming , he would have watched and his house would not have been vandalized .
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'would have watched' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('he') and an overt direct object ('his house'), satisfying the condition for ASTrns instantiation.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'watch' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent 'he') that performs the action of monitoring/observing, making it agentive rather than unaccusative.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'watched' licenses an internal argument (the implied object 'his house' or the event being monitored), making it transitive and thus not an unergative one-participant event.
AktAcc_explanation: The event 'would have watched' denotes a durative process of vigilant monitoring directed toward the goal of preventing theft, naturally modifiable by 'in X time' (e.g., 'watched for an hour'), and involves sustained action culminating in a defined protective result state.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'watch' denotes a durative activity that unfolds over measurable duration (monitoring/surveillance over time) rather than a punctual transition into a new state, and does not entail crossing a definable threshold.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'would have watched' denotes a dynamic, durative activity of sustained vigilance that unfolds over time without an inherent endpoint; it can be naturally modified by 'for X time' (e.g., 'would have watched for hours') and is homogeneous in nature, satisfying the criteria for AktAct.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'would have watched' entails a resulting change of state (preventing vandalism) and unfolds over a measurable duration rather than occurring as a single punctual occurrence, thus failing both criteria for semelfactive marking.
AktState_explanation: The conditional perfect 'would have watched' denotes a dynamic activity involving active volitional engagement and internal progression (the act of watching/monitoring), which entails eventive unfolding rather than a static state.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram 'would have watched' occurs in the apodosis of a counterfactual conditional introduced by 'if the master of the house had known', expressing an unfulfilled past action contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'would have watched' is not negated; the negation in the sentence ('would not have been vandalized') applies to a different clause and does not negate the eventuality of watching.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause contains the clausal past time anchor 'what time the thief was coming' introduced by 'what time', which functions as a temporal conjunction establishing a specific past time interval for the conditional event.
DtExp_explanation: The conditional perfect 'would have watched' entails a change of state (from not-watching to watching/being vigilant) with a resulting protective state that would persist at the reference time, making it non-experiential.
DtRes_explanation: The target is a counterfactual conditional ('would have watched') expressing a hypothetical past action that did not occur; it does not describe an actual change of state that holds at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The counterfactual conditional 'would have watched' expresses a continuous monitoring activity that would have held throughout the time span until the thief's arrival, denoting a non-terminating situation extending across a duration rather than a discrete bounded event.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The conditional 'would have watched' presents a hypothetical bounded event (the act of watching as a completed whole) that would have served as a discrete protective action in response to knowing the thief's arrival time, construing the situation as a single bounded occurrence rather than an ongoing or habitual state.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'would have watched' is a modal auxiliary construction (would + have + past participle) appearing in a counterfactual conditional clause ('if the master of the house had known...he would have watched'), presenting the situation as hypothetical and unrealized rather than asserted as fact.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'would have watched' includes a modal auxiliary 'would' preceding 'have', which instantiates TLTS according to the set_to_1_if rule for modal auxiliaries preceding 'have'.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'would have watched' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'he' as the semantic agent (doer), not a passive construction with the required 'have + been + past participle' morphology.

FullText: » Know this , if the master of the house had known what time the thief was coming , he would have watched and his house would not have been vandalized .
ASTrns_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive with the lexical verb 'vandalize' licensing both an external argument (agent—the thief) and an internal argument (patient—the house), satisfying the passive construction criterion for ASTrns.
ASUncc_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive (would not have been vandalized), and the lexical verb 'vandalize' licenses an external argument (an implicit agent who performs the vandalization), which triggers the set_to_0_if condition for passive clauses.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'vandalized' licenses an internal argument (the house functions as the theme/patient undergoing the change-of-state), and the passive construction places this internal argument in subject position rather than projecting an external argument.
AktAcc_explanation: Vandalism is a punctual boundary transition marking the onset of damage to a state, lacking a durative process phase inherent to the event itself; it denotes a near-instantaneous change-of-state rather than a gradual culmination toward a defined endpoint.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'vandalize' denotes a punctual transition into a new result state (the house becoming damaged/defaced), can be modified by time-point adverbials ('vandalized at midnight'), does not permit interruption ('one cannot stop being vandalized'), and lacks measurable internal duration, satisfying multiple set_to_1 criteria.
AktAct_explanation: The passive construction 'would not have been vandalized' denotes a bounded result state (the house being in a vandalized condition) with an inherent endpoint, not a dynamic durative activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'vandalize' entails a resulting change of state (the house transitions from non-vandalized to vandalized), which disqualifies it from being a semelfactive.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'would not have been vandalized' is a passive past perfect conditional expressing a bounded change-of-state event (the house transitioning from non-vandalized to vandalized state), which involves dynamic eventive unfolding incompatible with stative aspect.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The target gram occurs in the apodosis of a counterfactual conditional introduced by 'if the master had known' (protasis), expressing an unfulfilled past condition with modal perfect 'would not have been vandalized' indicating the house was actually vandalized.
ClNegPol_explanation: The auxiliary 'would' is negated with 'not' in 'would not have been vandalized', directly negating the eventuality of the house being vandalized.
DtDTAM_explanation: The target clause 'would not have been vandalized' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal temporal adverbial; the 'if' clause establishes a conditional rather than a specific past time anchor.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'vandalized' entails a change of state (house becoming damaged), and the resulting state of the house being vandalized would persist at the reference time, making this non-experiential rather than a life-history entry.
DtRes_explanation: The passive construction 'would not have been vandalized' describes a change of state (from non-vandalized to vandalized) where the resulting state (house remaining non-vandalized) would hold at the reference time, meeting the DtRes criteria for a resultative perfect.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a hypothetical transition into a new state (the house becoming vandalized or not) rather than a situation holding continuously over time; it focuses on the result of an event, not duration.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'would not have been vandalized' is a passive construction with 'have been' + past participle that presents a bounded, completed event (the vandalism as a whole) as a counterfactual outcome, construing the situation as a single bounded occurrence rather than an ongoing process.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'would not have been vandalized' appears in a counterfactual conditional construction ('if the master had known...he would have...') that presents the situation as hypothetical and unrealized rather than asserted as fact.
TLTS_explanation: The gram includes the modal auxiliary 'would' preceding 'have' plus 'been' followed by a past participle, satisfying both the modal and progressive perfect criteria for TLTS=1.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'would not have been vandalized' instantiates VcPass: it has the form auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle 'vandalized' of a transitive verb, the grammatical subject 'house' is the semantic patient (entity affected by vandalism), and the agent is omitted.

FullText: » His lord said : ‘ Well done , good and faithful servant . You have been faithful over a few things , so I will put you in charge of many things . Enter into the joy of your lord . ’
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'have been faithful' is a copular construction where 'been' links the subject to the predicative adjective 'faithful', introducing no internal argument (direct or indirect object), thus failing the set_to_1 criteria.
ASUncc_explanation: The target 'have been faithful' is a copular construction with 'be' where the sole argument 'You' functions as an internal argument linked to the predicative complement 'faithful', and no external argument is licensed.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'have been faithful' is a stative predicate that licenses an internal argument ('over a few things'), functioning as a complement rather than an unergative structure with only an external argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'have been faithful' denotes a stative condition or quality maintained over time rather than a dynamic event with an inherent process phase directed toward a defined endpoint or result state; it lacks the durative-process-to-completion structure required for AktAcc.
AktAch_explanation: The event 'have been faithful' unfolds over a measurable duration ('over a few things') rather than occurring at a single temporal boundary, and it denotes a durative state or activity without a punctual transition into a new result state.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have been faithful' denotes a stative property or result state (faithfulness maintained over time) rather than a dynamic, durative activity requiring sustained engagement; it lacks the homogeneous, interruptible quality of activities and represents a bounded evaluative state.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have been faithful' entails a resulting state of faithfulness maintained over time ('over a few things'), construing it as a durative state rather than a single punctual occurrence without consequent result.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'have been faithful' is morphologically marked with progressive aspect ('have been'), which entails dynamic unfolding over time and is incompatible with stative readings according to the set_to_0_if criteria.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'You have been faithful over a few things' presents a situation that has actually occurred in the actual world as a factual statement of past accomplishment, with no indication of counterfactuality or unfulfilled conditions.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have been faithful' is affirmative; no negator ('not', 'n't', 'never', or negative quantifier) negates this eventuality.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'You have been faithful over a few things' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal temporal adverbial that anchors the event to a specific past time interval; 'over a few things' is a spatial/domain modifier, not a temporal anchor.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'have been faithful' entails a persistent state of character/conduct that holds at the reference time, and this resulting state is directly relevant to the lord's consequent action ('I will put you in charge'), making it a state-resultative rather than an experiential perfect.
DtRes_explanation: The construction 'have been faithful' is a stative copula describing a continuous state rather than encoding a transition to a resulting state; it reports a persisting condition without entailing a change of state.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'You have been faithful over a few things' contains the duration adverbial 'over a few things' and denotes a non-terminating state of faithfulness that has held continuously from a past point to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'have been faithful' denotes a stative situation (faithfulness as a persistent state) without a construed boundary or transition, and the clause does not present the situation as a single bounded occurrence but rather as an ongoing state over the temporal span 'over a few things.'
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have been faithful' is asserted as a realized fact in the actual world within the lord's statement of evaluation, not presented as projected, hypothetical, or non-actual.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have been faithful' includes 'been' followed by an adjective functioning as a progressive-like construction, which instantiates the TLTS feature as it represents a present perfect progressive form rather than simple present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The complement of 'been' is the adjective 'faithful' (a non-verbal predicate), not a past participle of a transitive verb; 'been' functions as a lexical copula here, not a passive auxiliary.

FullText: » ‘ You should have put my money in the bank . I should have received my money back plus interest .
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'put' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('You') and an overt direct object ('my money'), satisfying the condition for ASTrns marking.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'put' licenses an external argument (the subject 'you') and introduces a direct object ('my money'), satisfying the set_to_0_if condition that the clause contains an overt direct object introduced by the target verb.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'put' licenses an internal argument ('my money') as a direct object, therefore it does not instantiate ASUnrg.
AktAcc_explanation: The event 'put my money in the bank' involves a directed action toward a spatial goal (the bank) with a defined completion state (money deposited), satisfying the criterion for accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'put' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (money moving from one location to another), naturally modifiable by time-point adverbials ('put it at noon'), and lacks measurable internal duration, representing a boundary crossing into a result state.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'put' denotes a bounded, punctual action with an inherent endpoint (placing money in a specific location), not a dynamic durative activity; it entails a defined result state and naturally takes 'in X time' modification, not 'for X time'.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'put' entails a resulting change of state (money transitioning from one location to another), which disqualifies it from being a semelfactive according to the set_to_0_if criteria.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'put' denotes a dynamic change-of-state event involving active progression and internal eventive unfolding (moving money from one location to another), which is incompatible with AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram 'should have put' occurs in the apodosis of a counterfactual conditional expressing an unfulfilled past obligation/expectation, where the event is understood not to have occurred in the actual world.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'should have put' is not negated; the negation ('not') does not appear in the sentence, and the modal construction expresses a counterfactual conditional rather than a negated eventuality.
DtDTAM_explanation: The target clause 'should have put' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal temporal adverbial that anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The negative conditional perfect 'should have put' functions as a counterfactual claim about a non-occurrence in the subject's history, fitting the experiential perfect pattern of asserting whether an event happened without focusing on a resulting state.
DtRes_explanation: The modal 'should have put' expresses a counterfactual or unrealized obligation rather than a change of state that holds at the reference time; it describes what ought to have happened, not what actually resulted in a persisting state.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete, bounded action (putting money in the bank) that should have occurred at a specific past point, not a situation holding continuously from a past starting point to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The modal 'should have put' construes a past transition event (putting money in a bank) as a bounded, completed whole that did not occur, presenting it as a single bounded occurrence with a construed endpoint rather than as ongoing or habitual.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'should have put' contains the modal auxiliary 'should' combined with 'have put' (perfect aspect), which presents a counterfactual or hypothetical situation that did not occur in reality, rather than asserting a realized fact.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'should have put' includes the modal auxiliary 'should' preceding 'have', which instantiates TLTS according to the set_to_1_if rule for modal auxiliaries preceding 'have'.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'should have put' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'You' as the semantic agent (doer), not a passive construction with 'have been' + past participle.

FullText: » ‘ You should have put my money in the bank . I should have received my money back plus interest .
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'should have received' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('I') and an overt direct object ('my money back plus interest'), satisfying the two-participant transitive pattern.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'receive' licenses an external argument (the subject 'I' as agent/experiencer), and the clause contains an overt direct object ('my money back plus interest'), so it does not instantiate the unaccusative feature.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'received' licenses an internal argument ('my money back plus interest'), which is a direct object, thus failing the ASUnrg criterion that requires no internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'receive' denotes a punctual boundary transition (the moment money comes into possession) without a durative process phase, making it an achievement rather than an accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'received' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (acquiring possession of money), naturally modifiable by time-point adverbials (e.g., 'at that moment'), and lacks measurable internal duration, representing a boundary crossing into a result state.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'receive' denotes a punctual, bounded perceptual event marking the onset of possession with a defined result state (having money), not a dynamic durative activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'receive' entails a transition into a resulting state (possession of money), which violates the semelfactive requirement of no resulting change of state.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'should have received' denotes a bounded perceptual/cognitive event marking the onset of awareness or acquisition of money, involving dynamic change and event completion, which is incompatible with stative aspect.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram 'should have received' occurs in the apodosis of a counterfactual conditional, expressing an unfulfilled past expectation where the money was not actually received, paired with the protasis 'You should have put my money in the bank.'
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'should have received' is not negated; the negation ('not') in the preceding clause applies to 'should have put', not to the target gram.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'I should have received my money back plus interest' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal temporal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'receive' entails a change of possession (acquiring money), and the clause focuses on the resulting state of having the money back plus interest, which would persist at the reference time if the event had occurred.
DtRes_explanation: The clause uses a counterfactual modal ('should have') expressing an unrealized hypothetical condition rather than an actual change of state that holds at the reference time; the money was never actually received, so no resulting state is entailed.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (receiving money back) that did not occur, focusing on a transition into a new state rather than a situation holding continuously over time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'I should have received my money back plus interest' presents a bounded completion event (receiving money) with a construed endpoint, using the 'have' + past participle form to assert a transition as a completed whole.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'should have received' contains the modal auxiliary 'should' combined with 'have received', presenting a counterfactual or hypothetical situation (what ought to have happened) rather than asserting a realized fact in the actual world.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'should have received' includes the modal auxiliary 'should' preceding 'have', which meets the set_to_1_if criterion for modal auxiliaries preceding 'have'.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'should have received' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'I' as the semantic agent (the one receiving money), not a passive construction with 'have been' + past participle.

FullText: When Jesus had finished talking , he said to his disciples :
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'talking' (gerund form of 'talk') does not introduce an overt internal argument; it is an activity reading where no affected patient is syntactically realized, matching the set_to_0_if criterion for objectless activity verbs.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'finished' licenses an external argument (Jesus as agent/initiator who deliberately completed the action of talking), thus it does not meet the unaccusative criteria which requires no external argument.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'talking' (gerund form of 'talk') encodes a one-participant event where the subject 'Jesus' functions as the external argument (agent/initiator) engaged in an activity that does not involve an internal argument, satisfying the criterion for ASUnrg.
AktAcc_explanation: The event 'finish talking' involves a durative process (the talking) directed toward a defined endpoint (completion of the talking), satisfying the accomplishment criteria of a process culminating in a result state.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'had finished talking' denotes completion of a durative process (talking unfolds over measurable duration), and naturally occurs as the complement of 'finish', which according to the set_to_0_if criteria indicates a durative process phase rather than a punctual state-change transition.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'had finished talking' denotes a bounded event with an inherent endpoint (completion of talking), satisfying the set_to_0_if criterion of entailing a specific, defined result state.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had finished talking' entails a resulting change of state (transition from talking to having completed talking), which disqualifies it from being a semelfactive.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'had finished talking' denotes a bounded dynamic event with an inherent endpoint (completion of the talking activity), involving internal eventive unfolding and change-of-state, which violates the stative requirement of AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'When Jesus had finished talking' uses past perfect to express temporal anteriority within a narrative sequence (the finishing occurred before the saying), not to indicate a counterfactual situation contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had finished talking' is not negated; there is no negator ('not', 'n't', 'never', or negative quantifier) that applies to this eventuality.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause contains the clausal past time anchor 'When', a temporal conjunction that explicitly anchors the event 'had finished talking' to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'had finished talking' denotes a bounded activity where the resulting state (the completion of talking) is relevant for the subsequent discourse action ('he said to his disciples'), making this a narrative progression event rather than an experiential life-history entry.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'finish talking' describes completion of an activity, not a change of state with a resulting condition that holds at the reference time; the clause uses past perfect to establish temporal sequence before 'he said', not to indicate a persisting resultant state.
DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'had finished talking' denotes a transition into a completed state (the finishing of talking) rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span, and there are no duration adverbials indicating continuous extension to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'had finished talking' presents the completion of a bounded event (finishing is a transition predicate) as a single whole, functioning as a discrete step in narrative sequence ('When...he said'), with the endpoint construed as achieved.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had finished talking' is a past perfect form asserting a realized event in the actual world (Jesus completed talking before speaking to disciples), with no modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical marking.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is morphologically past, making this a past perfect form rather than a simple present perfect, which satisfies the set_to_1_if condition for past auxiliary marking.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'had finished talking' is active voice with 'had' as a perfect auxiliary followed by a past participle, but the grammatical subject 'Jesus' is the semantic agent (doer) performing the action of talking, not the semantic patient; passive voice requires 'had been' + past participle with the subject as the affected entity.

FullText: » This ointment could have been sold for much and the money given to the poor . «
ASTrns_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive with 'could have been sold', and the lexical verb 'sell' licenses both an external argument (agent—implied 'by someone') and an internal argument (patient—'this ointment', the overt grammatical subject), satisfying the passive construction criterion.
ASUncc_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive (could have been sold), and the lexical verb 'sell' licenses an external argument (an implicit agent who would perform the selling), which triggers the set_to_0_if condition for passive clauses.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'sold' licenses an internal argument (the ointment functions as the theme/patient undergoing the change-of-state of being sold), and the subject does not function as an external agent but rather as the affected party in a passive construction.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'sell' denotes a durative commercial transaction directed toward a defined endpoint (transfer of goods for payment), naturally modifiable by 'in X time' (e.g., 'could have been sold in an hour'), instantiating the accomplishment feature AktAcc.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'sold' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (change of ownership/possession) that occurs at a definable temporal boundary, satisfying the achievement criterion despite the conditional modal framing.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'sold' denotes a bounded transaction with an inherent endpoint and result state (transfer of ownership), not a dynamic durative activity; the modal and perfect aspect do not change this fundamental telicity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'sold' entails a transition into a resulting state (change of ownership/possession), which disqualifies it from being a semelfactive; additionally, the conditional modal construction 'could have been' does not construe this as a single punctual occurrence but rather as a hypothetical durative transaction.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'sold' denotes a bounded change-of-state event involving dynamic transaction and transfer of ownership, which constitutes eventive unfolding incompatible with stative aspect.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram 'could have been sold' occurs in a counterfactual apodosis expressing an unfulfilled possibility contrary to what actually happened, marked by the modal perfect 'could have' which indicates an event that did not occur in the actual world.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'could have been sold' is not negated; the negation in the sentence ('not' in 'not to go' is absent here) does not apply to this gram, and no negative quantifiers, 'never', or 'neither...nor' constructions negate it.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal temporal adverbial that anchors the event to a specific past time interval; 'could have been sold' is a conditional/counterfactual construction without temporal anchoring.
DtExp_explanation: The negative perfect conditional 'could have been sold' presents a hypothetical event as part of what could have occurred in the subject's history, focusing on whether the event could have taken place at least once, without asserting a resulting state currently holds.
DtRes_explanation: The modal 'could have been' expresses a counterfactual or unrealized possibility rather than an actual change of state that holds at the reference time; the ointment was not actually sold, so no resulting state is entailed.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a hypothetical discrete event (selling the ointment) with a transition into a new state, not a situation holding continuously across a time span.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'could have been sold' is a passive perfect form asserting a bounded transition event (the selling of the ointment as a completed whole), which matches the set_to_1_if criterion for transition predicates construed as completed wholes.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram contains the modal auxiliary 'could' combined with 'have been sold', presenting a hypothetical, unrealized situation rather than an asserted fact.
TLTS_explanation: The gram includes modal auxiliary 'could' preceding 'have', which instantiates the TLTS feature according to the set_to_1_if rule for modal auxiliaries preceding 'have'.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'could have been sold' instantiates VcPass: it has the form modal + 'have' + 'been' + past participle 'sold' (transitive verb), the grammatical subject 'ointment' is the semantic patient (entity affected by selling), and the agent is omitted, satisfying all criteria for passive voice.

FullText: » It is written that the Son of man will depart . The man who betrays him will experience much grief . It would have been better if that man had not been born . «
ASTrns_explanation: The target 'would have been better' is a copular construction where 'be' links the subject to the predicative adjective 'better', introducing no internal argument and thus failing to instantiate ASTrns.
ASUncc_explanation: The copular verb 'be' in 'would have been better' does not license an external argument; the sole argument functions as an internal argument linked to the predicative complement 'better', conforming to the unaccusative copular pattern specified in the CRIs.
ASUnrg_explanation: The target 'would have been better' is a copular construction where 'been' licenses an internal argument (the predicate adjective 'better'), not a one-participant unergative verb with only an external argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The target 'would have been better' is a stative predicate expressing a punctual evaluative judgment or state comparison, not a dynamic durative event with an inherent process phase directed toward a defined endpoint.
AktAch_explanation: The target 'would have been better' is a stative predicate expressing a comparative evaluation or condition, not a punctual transition into a new result state; it lacks the dynamic change-of-state semantics required for AktAch classification.
AktAct_explanation: The target 'would have been better' is a stative predicate expressing a result state or condition rather than a dynamic, durative activity; it lacks dynamic unfolding and sustained engagement, and does not denote an ongoing action or natural activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The target 'would have been better' is a stative predicate expressing a state of comparison or evaluation, not a dynamic event, and therefore does not instantiate the semelfactive feature which requires a punctual dynamic occurrence.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'would have been better' denotes a stable relational/evaluative state (a comparative condition of superiority) that holds over time without dynamic change or internal progression, fitting the stative feature AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram 'would have been better' occurs in the apodosis of a counterfactual conditional ('if that man had not been born'), expressing an unfulfilled past wish where the event is understood not to have occurred.
ClNegPol_explanation: The negation 'not' in the conditional clause 'if that man had not been born' negates the embedded verb 'had been born', not the target gram 'would have been better', which expresses a counterfactual comparison without direct negation.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'would have been better' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal temporal adverbial anchoring the event to a specific past time interval; the 'if' clause is conditional, not temporal.
DtExp_explanation: The conditional perfect 'would have been better' expresses a counterfactual state of affairs (a better condition) rather than presenting an event as part of life history; it introduces a resulting state in a hypothetical scenario, not an experiential occurrence.
DtRes_explanation: The target is a stative copula construction ('would have been better') that describes a hypothetical state rather than encoding a transition to a resulting state that holds at the reference time; it expresses a counterfactual condition without entailing a current change of state.
DtUniv_explanation: The target expresses a counterfactual conditional about a hypothetical state ('it would have been better if...') rather than a situation holding continuously from a past point to the reference time, and contains no duration adverbials or habitual series indicators.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'would have been better' expresses a counterfactual stative situation (a state of being better) without a construed transition or bounded occurrence; it denotes an unbounded state contrary to fact rather than a completed event.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'would have been better' contains the modal auxiliary 'would' combined with the perfect infinitive 'have been', presenting a counterfactual/hypothetical evaluation of a non-realized situation rather than asserting an actual fact.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'would have been better' includes the modal auxiliary 'would' preceding 'have', which satisfies the set_to_1_if condition for modal auxiliaries preceding 'have'.
VcPass_explanation: The complement of 'been' is the adjective 'better' (a non-verbal predicate), making 'been' a lexical copula rather than a passive auxiliary; this is a copular construction, not a passive voice construction.

FullText: » It is written that the Son of man will depart . The man who betrays him will experience much grief . It would have been better if that man had not been born . «
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'had not been born' is a passive construction with no overt internal argument; 'born' functions as a predicative complement describing the subject's state rather than introducing an affected patient argument.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'be born' is a change-of-state event where the sole argument (that man) functions as an internal argument (theme/patient) undergoing the event of coming into existence, and no external argument is licensed.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'born' denotes a change-of-state/coming-into-being event where the sole argument (that man) functions as an internal argument (theme/patient) undergoing the state of being born, not as an external argument initiating an action.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'be born' denotes a punctual boundary transition marking entry into existence, lacking a durative process phase and thus instantiating an achievement rather than an accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'be born' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (existence) that occurs at a definable temporal boundary, naturally modified by time-point adverbials (e.g., 'born at noon'), lacks measurable internal duration, and cannot be interrupted as an ongoing process.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'be born' denotes a single punctual event marking a defined result state (existence following birth), not a dynamic durative activity; it lacks sustained dynamic engagement and represents a bounded occurrence with an inherent endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'be born' entails a transition into a resulting state (existence/life) and is not a punctual occurrence without consequent result, thus it does not instantiate AktSemel.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'had not been born' is morphologically marked as progressive perfect aspect ('had been') combined with a past participle, encoding a bounded change-of-state event (birth) with a definite endpoint, which entails dynamic eventive unfolding incompatible with stative readings.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram occurs in the protasis of a counterfactual conditional ('if that man had not been born') paired with the apodosis 'It would have been better', expressing an unfulfilled past condition contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is negated with 'not' in 'had not been born', directly negating the eventuality of the man being born.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'had not been born' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal temporal adverbial that anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: This is a negative Perfect expressing the absence of an event (birth) from the subject's life history, which directly matches the set_to_1_if criterion for negative Perfects functioning as claims about absence in the subject's history.
DtRes_explanation: The clause is negated ('had not been born'), so no transition to a resulting state is entailed; the CRI explicitly states to set to 0 when 'the clause negates the occurrence of the event.'
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'had not been born' describes a transition into a state (non-existence/non-birth) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, and expresses a counterfactual condition rather than a duration extending to reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause is negated with 'not' in a counterfactual conditional expressing an unrealized state ('had not been born'), which presents an open, non-bounded situation rather than a completed transition.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had not been born' appears in a counterfactual conditional construction ('It would have been better if that man had not been born'), which presents the situation as hypothetical and contrary to fact rather than realized.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary is morphologically past ('had') rather than present-tense 'have/has', which instantiates the TLTS feature.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had not been born' instantiates VcPass: it has the form auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle of transitive verb 'born', the grammatical subject 'that man' is the semantic patient (the entity affected by the event of birth), and the agent is omitted.

FullText: As he spoke , Judas , one of the twelve , followed by a crowd carrying swords and spears , approached him . The chief priest and elders of the people had sent them .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'had sent' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('The chief priest and elders of the people') and an overt direct object ('them'), satisfying the transitive pattern with both external and internal arguments.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'had sent' licenses an external argument (the chief priest and elders as agent) and introduces a direct object (them), satisfying the set_to_0_if condition that the clause contains an overt grammatical object introduced by the target verb.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'had sent' licenses an internal argument ('them'), making it transitive and thus not an unergative one-participant event.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'had sent' denotes a punctual act of dispatching that constitutes a single boundary transition into the state of having been sent, lacking a durative process phase or inherent endpoint that defines completion.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'had sent' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (the act of sending), which can be modified by a specific time-point adverbial and does not permit interruption as an ongoing process phase.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'had sent' denotes a completed action with a specific result state (them being sent), lacks dynamic durative unfolding, and cannot naturally be modified by 'for X time' without implying incompleteness, making it a bounded accomplishment rather than an activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had sent' entails a resulting change of state (the priests caused them to be sent/dispatched), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status according to the set_to_0_if criteria.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'had sent' denotes a bounded dynamic event (sending) involving change-of-state and internal eventive unfolding, which is incompatible with AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'The chief priest and elders of the people had sent them' uses past perfect to express temporal anteriority within a narrative (the sending occurred before the approaching), not to indicate a counterfactual situation contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had sent' is not negated; it expresses a realized past event without any negator ('not', 'n't', 'never', or negative quantifier) directly modifying it.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'The chief priest and elders of the people had sent them' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal temporal adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'had sent' entails a change of possession/agency (the priests caused the crowd to be sent) with a resulting state (the crowd is now present as agents of the priests) that is relevant to the narrative progression and current discourse context.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'had sent' describes a past action of sending that does not entail a resulting state currently in effect; it reports a prior interaction without a persisting state (the sending itself is complete but no resulting condition holds at the reference time).
DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'had sent' denotes a discrete bounded event (the act of sending) completed before the reference time, not a continuous situation holding throughout a time span.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'had sent them' presents a completed bounded event (the sending of the crowd) as a single whole prior to the narrative sequence of approaching, construing the transition/action as a finished occurrence rather than an ongoing or stative situation.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had sent' asserts a realized past event in the actual world (the chief priests and elders had already sent the crowd), with no modal, conditional, counterfactual, or hypothetical context present.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is morphologically past, making this past perfect rather than simple present perfect, which satisfies the set_to_1_if condition for past auxiliary marking.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had sent' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'The chief priest and elders' as the semantic agent (doers) and 'them' as the grammatical object (semantic patient); it lacks the passive auxiliary sequence 'have/had' + 'been' + past participle.

FullText: » This has taken place so that the writings of the prophets will come true . « Then all his disciples left him .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'take place' is an idiom functioning as an intransitive verb with no overt internal argument (direct or indirect object); it describes an event occurring without an affected patient.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'take place' denotes an occurrence/event that happens without licensing an external argument; the sole argument 'this' functions as an internal argument (theme) undergoing the event, matching the set_to_1_if criterion for existence/occurrence verbs.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'take place' denotes an event of coming-into-being or occurrence without projecting an external argument (agent/initiator); the subject 'this' functions as a theme/patient undergoing the event rather than as an external argument initiating an action.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'take place' denotes a punctual boundary transition marking the occurrence of an event without a durative process phase leading to completion, similar to 'arrive' or 'happen'.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'take place' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (an event occurring at a definable temporal boundary) and can be naturally modified by time-point adverbials (e.g., 'took place at noon'), meeting the criteria for achievement aspect.
AktAct_explanation: The phrase 'has taken place' denotes a bounded, completed event with an inherent endpoint (the occurrence of an event), not a dynamic, durative activity that unfolds over time without inherent completion.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has taken place' entails a transition into a resulting state (the prophets' writings coming true as a consequence), which disqualifies it from being a semelfactive according to the set_to_0_if criteria.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'take place' denotes a bounded dynamic event with an inherent endpoint and internal eventive unfolding, and the present perfect progressive form 'has taken place' marks completion of this event, making it incompatible with stative readings.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause presents 'has taken place' as an actual event that occurred in the narrative world, with no counterfactual marking, conditional structure, or indication that the situation is contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has taken place' is not negated; the sentence affirms that an event has occurred, and no negator (not, n't, never, or negative quantifier) applies to this verb.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'has taken place' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal past time adverbial; 'so that' is a purposive conjunction, not a temporal anchor.
DtExp_explanation: The clause 'has taken place' introduces a completed event whose resulting state (that the event occurred) is relevant for the subsequent discourse explaining why the prophets' writings will come true, making it non-experiential rather than a life-history entry.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'take place' describes an event that has occurred and the resulting state (that the event has happened) holds at the reference time, meeting the criterion for a change of state with a persisting result.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (something taking place) rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span, and the verb 'taken place' entails a punctual transition into a new state.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'has taken place' presents a bounded, completed event (a transition from not-occurring to occurring) as a single whole, without internal temporal development, and functions as a discrete step in the narrative sequence.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has taken place' is asserted as a realized fact in the actual world using present perfect tense, with no modal, conditional, future, or wish context applying.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has taken place' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by past participle 'taken', with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'has taken place' is active voice with 'taken place' functioning as a phrasal verb idiom (not a passive participle), where the grammatical subject 'This' is the semantic agent/experiencer, and there is no 'been' auxiliary or by-phrase characteristic of passive voice.

FullText: Then the high priest ripped his robes apart and said : » He has said evil against God ! Why do we need more witnesses ? You have heard his words of blasphemy ! «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'have heard' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('You') and an overt direct object ('his words of blasphemy'), satisfying the two-participant transitive pattern with both external and internal arguments.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'heard' licenses an external argument (the experiencer 'you' who performs the act of hearing), and the clause contains a direct object ('his words of blasphemy'), so it does not instantiate ASUncc.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'heard' licenses an internal argument ('his words of blasphemy'), making it transitive and thus not an unergative one-participant event.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'have heard' denotes a punctual perceptual event—a near-instantaneous transition into awareness upon encountering the utterance—lacking a durative process phase or inherent endpoint, thus classifying as an achievement rather than an accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have heard' denotes a punctual perceptual transition into awareness (hearing/perceiving the blasphemous words at a specific moment), naturally modifiable by time-point adverbials, with no measurable internal duration, meeting the criteria for achievement aspect.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have heard' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the onset of awareness of 'his words of blasphemy,' which is a single completed occurrence rather than a dynamic, durative situation with sustained engagement.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have heard' entails a resulting change of state (transition from not knowing to knowing the content of the words), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have heard' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the completion of awareness (hearing blasphemous words), which encodes eventive change rather than a static stative condition.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'You have heard his words of blasphemy' presents a situation that actually occurred in the narrative world (the witnesses did hear the blasphemous words), with no indication of counterfactuality or unfulfilled conditions.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have heard' is not negated; it is a positive assertion in an imperative clause, and the negation 'not' in the sentence applies to the embedded infinitive 'to go' (implied in context), not to the target gram itself.
DtDTAM_explanation: The target clause 'You have heard his words of blasphemy' contains no overt temporal adverbial anchoring the event to a specific past time interval; 'Then' at the sentence start modifies the narrative frame, not this clause.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'have heard' denotes perception where the focus is on the occurrence of the experience (hearing the blasphemous words) at least once in the subject's life history, rather than on a resulting state.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have heard' is a perception event that does not entail a resulting state currently in effect; it reports a past interaction (hearing words) without a persisting change of state that holds at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'You have heard his words of blasphemy' describes a discrete, bounded event of perceiving/receiving information at a specific moment, not a continuous situation holding over a time span.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'have heard' denotes a stative situation (perception/knowledge state) without a construed boundary or transition; the clause does not present a bounded occurrence but rather asserts an ongoing state of knowledge accessible to the addressees.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have heard' is a present perfect asserting a realized fact in the actual world—the witnesses have actually heard the blasphemous words—with no modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have heard' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by past participle 'heard' with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form.
VcPass_explanation: The form 'have heard' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'You' as the semantic agent (the one who heard), not a passive construction with 'have been' + past participle.

FullText: He said : » I have done wrong in giving a righteous man to you . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'have done wrong' lacks an overt internal argument (direct or indirect object); 'wrong' functions as a predicative complement describing the action, not as an affected patient object.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'done' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent 'I' who performed the wrongdoing), making it unergative/transitive rather than unaccusative.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'do wrong' licenses an internal argument ('wrong' functions as a cognate object/complement), and the participial phrase 'in giving a righteous man to you' further indicates a complex predicate with internal semantic content, thus failing the ASUnrg criterion of having no internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'do wrong' denotes a punctual moral transgression or boundary transition into wrongdoing without a durative process phase leading to completion, fitting the achievement category rather than accomplishment.
